narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile in Year 3 of school, where your ranking in the 2nd decile stood out as a major push toward predicting failure—far below the typical 6th decile average among students who passed the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also at the 2nd level compared to passers' average around the 6th, which further reinforced the model's concern about early struggles carrying forward. Your upper-middle family income bracket played a smaller but still notable role, nudging the prediction toward failure since it didn't align as strongly with the profiles of successful bar takers as other factors might have. While your LSAT score of 34 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 were below the averages of 37.27 and 3.24 for those who passed, and your choice of full-time enrollment is common among successes, they weren't the primary drivers here. Overall, your profile contrasted sharply with admitted students, who typically showed higher decile rankings in both early and later years, signaling consistent academic strength. These early and mid-school performance indicators were the top factors tipping the scales against you, even with your solid family background and full-time commitment. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially as a driven applicant with these qualifications. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and focus on strategies to improve your class rankings in future coursework, perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Building a stronger undergraduate-like GPA in any additional classes could also help reshape your profile. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying—your path isn't closed, just needs targeted refinement.
